verbatim_text: You could also have archived papers as one of these subsets of your overall data set that represents the skill to read papers or produce papers or something. Finally, you could have Wikipedia as a skill that's associated with, of course, all of the knowledge data that's on Wikipedia. And so, okay, that's great. We have that. So now we also need to understand how these skills are related to one another, because remember, we have these dependencies between skills. So some examples could be you have Wikipedia and you need to learn Wikipedia before you learn archive. Perhaps you're reading a very complex math paper and you need knowledge of the terms that are used in order to actually learn from that paper. Another example could be that you maybe need to train on a bunch of different stack over for the questions in order to understand why a GitHub pull request was merged. And while that's great, we don't necessarily have access to those dependencies right off the bat. And perhaps also if we had intuition about what things might be related to one another, those intuitions might be wrong because models also learn shortcuts and so on.
